games.explanation: Bradish's line looks like a buzzsaw until you notice the asterisk on the sample, and the asterisk is that lefties have been roughing him up while righties have made him look like Cy Young. The Athletics happen to roll out a lineup built around left-handed thump, with Kurtz leading a group that's been crushing right-handed pitching all year, and Bradish is walking nearly 13% of the lefties he faces. Lopez is no ace, but he doesn't need to be when the park plays neutral and the projected score lands inside a run. Getting plus money on the better-projected team in a coin-flip game is the kind of price that doesn't need a sermon, and +120 is doing the talking.

The market is buying Connelly Early's shiny rookie line at face value, ignoring that 98 batters faced is a sample size, not a scouting report. Tampa rolls out a stack of right-handed bats who actually punish lefties, with Aranda and Diaz both posting xwOBAs north of .390 against southpaws, and Caminero doesn't particularly care which arm he's hitting against. The Red Sox lineup is fine but not the kind of group that runs away from anyone, and the sim has this game inside a single run. Getting plus money on the better team in a coin flip is the entire pitch, and +118 is generous for a road favorite in disguise.

Nolan McLean's surface numbers look like an ace's, but the split underneath tells a different story: right-handed bats have been getting to him in a way lefties haven't, and Arizona happens to roll out a lineup full of them. Carroll, Marte, and Perdomo are exactly the profile that's tagged him for a .310 xwOBA, and McLean's walk rate climbs fast when he can't trust the zone. Ryne Nelson isn't a star, but he's the kind of strike-thrower who keeps a roof game from spiraling, and the simulated score has this as a coin flip the market is pricing as a clear favorite. Getting plus money on the better team is the whole pitch, and +116 is a gift you take.

Griffin Canning gets penciled in as a Padres starter and the market reflexively prices San Diego like the better team, but the underlying numbers say he's been giving up the same quality of contact as a back-end Cardinals arm. Canning walks nearly 11 percent of hitters and gets barreled up at a rate that does not survive a lineup with Ivan Herrera in it, and Herrera against this kind of command-shaky righty is the matchup of the night. McGreevy is not flashy, but he pounds the zone and lets a good defense work, which is exactly the recipe in a park that suppresses runs. Getting +130 on the team my numbers make a small favorite is the entire reason to write the ticket.
